🔭 Science · July 6, 2026 · 1 min read

Surgeons performed the world's first whole-eye transplant

For centuries, transplanting a whole human eye was considered impossible — the organ is too delicate, its connection to the brain too complex. Then a surgical team at NYU Langone Health did it, transplanting an entire eye as part of a face transplant for a power-line worker who survived a severe electrical accident.

Well over a year on, the transplanted eye has remained healthy, with blood flow to the retina — something many experts doubted could be achieved. The patient's sight has not returned yet, but the door is open: medicine finally has living proof that the human eye can survive transplantation, the essential first step toward one day restoring vision to the blind.

#medicine#surgery#breakthrough
Source: NYU Langone Health

This summary was written by our AI editor from the source's reporting. We always credit and link the original.

More good news

The ozone layer is healing — on track for full recovery

UN scientists confirm the ozone layer is steadily recovering thanks to a worldwide ban on harmful chemicals — proof cooperation works.

🔭 Science · July 4, 2026 · 1 min read

The Iberian lynx is back from the brink of extinction

Once down to fewer than a hundred animals, the world's rarest wild cat has multiplied more than twentyfold and is no longer endangered.

🌿 Nature · July 7, 2026 · 1 min read

Millions of children are now protected by malaria vaccines

Two safe, effective vaccines are rolling out across Africa, reaching millions of children in the fight against one of humanity's oldest killers.

❤️ Health · July 6, 2026 · 1 min read
